title: Copy with random pointer
description: Copy a linked list with random pointers

examples.input: Original list: 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> null, random pointers: 1.random = 3, 2.random = 1, 3.random = 2
examples.explanation: Step 1: Create new list by copying values and next pointers. New list: node1(1) -> node2(2) -> node3(3) -> null. Random pointers are null initially. Step 2: For original node1, random points to original node3. Traverse original list to find node3 at position 3 (1-indexed). Traverse new list to position 3 and set new node1.random = new node3. Step 3: For original node2, random points to original node1 at position 1. Traverse new list to position 1 and set new node2.random = new node1. Step 4: For original node3, random points to original node2 at position 2. Traverse new list to position 2 and set new node3.random = new node2. Result: new list with correct random pointers.

examples.input: Original list: 5 -> null, random pointer: 5.random = null
examples.explanation: Step 1: Create new list: node1(5) -> null. Step 2: For original node5, random is null, so set new node5.random = null without traversal. Result: single node copied correctly.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Create a new linked list by copying each node's value and next pointer, then for each new node, traverse the original list to find the corresponding random pointer node and link it. This is straightforward but inefficient due to repeated traversals.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Deep Copy Linked List with Random Pointers using Brute Force O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of copying a linked list with random pointers using the brute force approach.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to copying a linked list with random pointers involves two main phases. First, we create a new linked list by copying each node's value and next pointer sequentially. This gives us a new list with correct values and next pointers, but random pointers are not set yet. Second, for each node in the new list, we need to set its random pointer. Since we don't have a mapping between original and new nodes, we must traverse the original list to find the node that corresponds to the random pointer of the current original node. Once found, we traverse the new list to the same position and set the random pointer accordingly. This results in O(n²) time complexity because for each of the n nodes, we may traverse up to n nodes to find the matching random pointer. The space complexity is O(n) for the new list. The intuition is straightforward: we're manually matching positions between the original and new lists by index, which is inefficient but conceptually simple. This approach is foundational for understanding why more efficient methods (like using hash maps) are necessary. Common mistakes include not handling null random pointers correctly, off-by-one errors in traversal, or incorrectly assuming nodes are unique by value. Always verify that you're matching nodes by position, not by value, since values could be duplicated.

examples.input: Original list: 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> null, random pointers: 1.random = 3, 2.random = 1, 3.random = 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Create new list by copying values and next pointers. New list: node1(1) -> node2(2) -> node3(3) -> null. Random pointers are null initially. Step 2: For original node1, random points to original node3. Traverse original list to find node3 at position 3 (1-indexed). Traverse new list to position 3 and set new node1.random = new node3. Step 3: For original node2, random points to original node1 at position 1. Traverse new list to position 1 and set new node2.random = new node1. Step 4: For original node3, random points to original node2 at position 2. Traverse new list to position 2 and set new node3.random = new node2. Result: new list with correct random pointers.

examples.input: Original list: 5 -> null, random pointer: 5.random = null
examples.trace: Step 1: Create new list: node1(5) -> null. Step 2: For original node5, random is null, so set new node5.random = null without traversal. Result: single node copied correctly.

testCases.input: Original list: 7 -> 7 -> 7 -> null, random: first 7.random=third 7, second 7.random=first 7, third 7.random=second 7
testCases.expected: Copied list: 7 -> 7 -> 7 -> null, random: first 7.random=third 7, second 7.random=first 7, third 7.random=second 7

testCases.input: Original list: 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> null, random: all random pointers are null
testCases.expected: Copied list: 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> null, random: all null

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Setting Random Pointers by Position Matching
ladders.desc: The core function that sets random pointers in the new list by finding corresponding nodes in the original list.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach. After creating a new list with values and next pointers, we need to set the random pointers. For each node in the original list, we identify the node its random pointer points to. Since we don't have a direct mapping, we determine the position (index) of that target node in the original list by traversing from the head. Then, we traverse the new list to the same position and set the random pointer of the current new node to that node. The intuition is that the new list is a positional copy of the original, so nodes at the same index correspond to each other. This works because the next pointers create a consistent order. The algorithm involves nested loops: an outer loop iterates over each node in the original list, and an inner loop finds the position of the random target. Time complexity is O(n²) in the worst case, as each node may require a full traversal. Space complexity is O(1) for this sub-routine alone, excluding the new list. This feeds into L0 by completing the copy process after the initial list creation. Common mistakes include not handling null random pointers (skip traversal), incorrect position counting (use 0-indexed or 1-indexed consistently), or assuming unique values. Always use traversal to find positions, not value matching.

examples.input: Original list: A -> B -> C -> null, random: A.random=C, B.random=A, C.random=B. New list (values copied): A' -> B' -> C' -> null, random pointers unset.
examples.trace: Step 1: For original A, random points to C. Traverse original: A (pos 0), B (pos 1), C (pos 2). C is at position 2. Traverse new list to position 2: A' (pos 0), B' (pos 1), C' (pos 2). Set A'.random = C'. Step 2: For original B, random points to A at position 0. Traverse new list to position 0: A'. Set B'.random = A'. Step 3: For original C, random points to B at position 1. Traverse new list to position 1: B'. Set C'.random = B'.

examples.input: Original list: X -> Y -> null, random: X.random=null, Y.random=X. New list: X' -> Y' -> null.
examples.trace: Step 1: For original X, random is null, so set X'.random = null without traversal. Step 2: For original Y, random points to X at position 0. Traverse new list to position 0: X'. Set Y'.random = X'.

testCases.input: Original: 1->2->3->null, random: all null, new list: 1'->2'->3'->null

ladders.title: Core Logic — Position-based Node Correspondence in Linked Lists
ladders.desc: Understanding how to match nodes between two linked lists based on their positional index.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of the brute force approach is that we can establish a correspondence between nodes in the original and new linked lists based on their position (index) from the head. Since we create the new list by sequentially copying nodes in the order of the original list, the node at position i in the new list corresponds to the node at position i in the original list. This allows us to set random pointers by finding the position of the target node in the original list and then accessing the node at the same position in the new list. The intuition relies on the fact that linked lists are linear structures where order is defined by next pointers, so copying preserves this order. This technique is fundamental but inefficient because finding the position requires traversal. The algorithm involves two helper functions: one to find the position of a node in a list (by traversing from head until the node is found) and another to get the node at a given position in a list (by traversing from head to that index). Time complexity for finding position is O(n) per node, leading to O(n²) overall. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L1 by providing the mechanism for setting random pointers. Common mistakes include not handling null nodes correctly, off-by-one errors in index calculation, or assuming nodes are unique by value. Always traverse from the head each time to ensure accurate position matching.

examples.input: Original list: P -> Q -> R -> null. Node R is at position 2 (0-indexed).
examples.trace: Start at head P (position 0). Move to next: Q (position 1). Move to next: R (position 2). Found R, so position is 2.

examples.input: New list: P' -> Q' -> R' -> null. Get node at position 1.
examples.trace: Start at head P' (position 0). Move to next: Q' (position 1). Reached position 1, return Q'.

ladders.title: Building Block — Traversing a Linked List to Find Node Position
ladders.desc: The sub-problem of determining the index of a given node in a linked list by traversing from the head.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential for the brute force approach because we need to find the position (index) of a node in the original list to match it with the corresponding node in the new list. The intuition is simple: since linked lists are sequential, we can start at the head and move forward, counting steps until we reach the target node. This works because each node is connected via next pointers, creating a linear order. The algorithm involves initializing a counter to 0 and a current pointer to the head. Then, while the current pointer is not null, check if the current node is the target node (by reference comparison, not value). If yes, return the counter. If not, move to the next node and increment the counter. If the target is not found (e.g., it's null or not in the list), return -1 or a sentinel value. Time complexity is O(n) in the worst case, as we may traverse the entire list. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the position-finding function needed for node correspondence. Common mistakes include comparing nodes by value instead of reference (which fails if values are duplicated), not handling null targets correctly, or off-by-one errors in the counter. Always use reference equality to ensure accuracy.

examples.input: List: 10 -> 20 -> 30 -> null, target node: node with value 20
examples.trace: Start at head 10, position 0. Not target (value 10 ≠ 20). Move to next 20, position 1. Is target (value 20 = 20). Return position 1.

examples.input: List: 5 -> null, target node: node with value 5
examples.trace: Start at head 5, position 0. Is target. Return position 0.

testCases.input: List: 7->7->null, target: second node with value 7

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through a Linked List
ladders.desc: The fundamental skill of traversing each node in a linked list sequentially using a while loop.
ladders.explanation: Iterating through a linked list is a basic operation that underpins many algorithms, including the brute force copy approach. The intuition is that a linked list is a chain of nodes where each node points to the next, so we can start at the head and repeatedly move to the next node until we reach the end (null). This allows us to access each node exactly once in order. The algorithm involves initializing a current pointer to the head of the list. Then, use a while loop that continues as long as current is not null. Inside the loop, you can perform operations on the current node (e.g., copy its value). After processing, update current to current.next to move to the next node. This traversal has a time complexity of O(n) and space complexity of O(1). It feeds into L3 by providing the mechanism to find a node's position through linear search. Common mistakes include infinite loops by not updating current, accessing null pointers, or losing the head reference. Always ensure the loop condition checks for null and that current is updated correctly.

examples.input: List: 3 -> 6 -> 9 -> null
examples.trace: Start: current = head (node 3). Loop: current not null, process node 3. Update current = current.next (node 6). Loop: process node 6. Update current = node 9. Loop: process node 9. Update current = null. Loop ends.

examples.input: List: 1 -> null
examples.trace: Start: current = head (node 1). Loop: process node 1. Update current = null. Loop ends.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: The basic concept of what a linked list node is and how it connects to other nodes.
ladders.explanation: A linked list node is a fundamental data structure that forms the building block of a linked list. Each node typically contains two parts: data (or value) and a pointer (or reference) to the next node in the sequence. In the context of copying with random pointers, nodes have an additional pointer called 'random' that can point to any node in the list or be null. The intuition is that nodes are like boxes with content (the value) and arrows (the pointers) that link them together. The 'next' pointer creates the main sequence of the list, while the 'random' pointer adds extra connections that can jump around. Understanding this structure is crucial because copying involves creating new nodes with the same values and setting up both types of pointers correctly. This concept feeds into L4 by defining what we traverse when iterating. Common mistakes include confusing pointers with values, not initializing pointers to null, or misunderstanding how pointers reference other nodes. Always visualize nodes as objects with fields for value, next, and random.

examples.input: Create a node with value 5, next pointing to null, random pointing to null.
examples.trace: Define a node object. Set its value field to 5. Set its next pointer to null (indicating no next node). Set its random pointer to null (indicating no random connection).

examples.input: Two nodes: node A with value 1, next points to node B, random points to node B. Node B with value 2, next points to null, random points to node A.
examples.trace: Create node A, set value=1. Create node B, set value=2. Set A.next = B to link A to B. Set A.random = B to point A's random to B. Set B.next = null as end of list. Set B.random = A to point B's random to A.

testCases.input: Node value=1, next points to another node with value=2, random=null
Node value=3, next=null, random points to itself
Two nodes: first value=5, next=second, random=second; second value=6, next=null, random=first
testCases.expected: First: value=5, next=second, random=second; second: value=6, next=null, random=first

testCases.input: Three nodes: A value=1, next=B, random=C; B value=2, next=C, random=A; C value=3, next=null, random=B
testCases.expected: A: value=1, next=B, random=C; B: value=2, next=C, random=A; C: value=3, next=null, random=B

testCases.input: Node value=4, next points to node with value=4, random=null
Node value=9, next=null, random points to node with value=9

approaches.name: HashMap O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a hash map to store mappings from original nodes to new nodes. In the first pass, create new nodes and build the map; in the second pass, use the map to set random pointers by looking up the corresponding new nodes. This avoids repeated searches.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Deep Copy Linked List with Random Pointers Using HashMap
ladders.desc: Complete solution to copy a linked list where each node has next and random pointers using a hash map approach.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to create a mapping between original nodes and their corresponding new copies, which allows us to efficiently set up the random pointers without repeated searches. The random pointer in a linked list can point to any node (including null or itself), making a simple traversal insufficient because we need to maintain the exact relationships. By using a hash map, we can store each original node as a key and its new copy as the value. In the first pass, we create new nodes for each original node and populate the map. In the second pass, we traverse the original list again, using the map to look up the new nodes corresponding to the original next and random pointers, and set these pointers in the new list. This ensures that the new list is a deep copy with identical structure. The time complexity is O(n) because we make two passes through the list, each of O(n) time. The space complexity is O(n) due to the hash map storing n entries. Connection to higher levels: This builds on the core logic of using a hash map for node mapping (L1) and the building block of linked list traversal (L2). Common mistakes include not handling null pointers correctly (e.g., when random is null), forgetting to set next pointers in the second pass, or incorrectly assuming the list is circular. To avoid these, always check for null before accessing map entries and ensure both passes cover all nodes. Real code pattern: Initialize an empty hash map. First pass: traverse original list, for each node, create a new node with same value, store mapping in map. Second pass: traverse original list again, for each node, set new node's next to map[original.next] and new node's random to map[original.random]. Return map[head] as the new head.

examples.input: List: 1->2->3->null, random pointers: 1.random=3, 2.random=1, 3.random=2
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty map {}. Step 2: First pass - Create new nodes: For node 1 (val=1), create new node N1 (val=1), map[1]=N1. For node 2 (val=2), create N2, map[2]=N2. For node 3 (val=3), create N3, map[3]=N3. Step 3: Second pass - Set pointers: For node 1, N1.next = map[2] = N2, N1.random = map[3] = N3. For node 2, N2.next = map[3] = N3, N2.random = map[1] = N1. For node 3, N3.next = map[null] = null, N3.random = map[2] = N2. Result: N1->N2->N3->null with N1.random=N3, N2.random=N1, N3.random=N2.

examples.input: List: A->B->null, random pointers: A.random=B, B.random=A
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize map {}. Step 2: First pass - Create new nodes: For node A (val='A'), create NA, map[A]=NA. For node B (val='B'), create NB, map[B]=NB. Step 3: Second pass - Set pointers: For node A, NA.next = map[B] = NB, NA.random = map[B] = NB. For node B, NB.next = map[null] = null, NB.random = map[A] = NA. Result: NA->NB->null with NA.random=NB, NB.random=NA.

testCases.expected: Copied list: 1->null, random points to itself
Copied list: 1->2->3->null with each node random points to itself

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Mapping Original Nodes to New Nodes Using HashMap
ladders.desc: Create and use a hash map to store correspondences between original and copied nodes.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to copy a linked list with random pointers, we need a way to quickly find the new copy of any original node when setting up pointers. A hash map provides O(1) average-time lookups, making it efficient. This sub-routine involves two main actions: populating the map during node creation and querying it during pointer assignment. In the first action, as we traverse the original list, for each node, we create a new node with the same value and store the pair (original node, new node) in the map. This ensures every original node has a corresponding entry. In the second action, when we need to set the next or random pointer for a new node, we look up the map using the original node's next or random pointer to get the new node reference. This avoids having to search through the new list, which would be O(n) per node. The time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n) for populating and O(1) per lookup, but overall integrated into the full solution. Space complexity is O(n) for storing n entries. Connection to L0: This is the core mechanism that enables the full solution to work efficiently; without it, we'd need inefficient searches. Common mistakes include not handling null keys in the map (e.g., when original.next is null), which should map to null, or forgetting to add nodes to the map during creation. To avoid, always add an entry for each original node, and check for null before map access. Real code pattern: map = {}; for node in original_list: new_node = Node(node.val); map[node] = new_node; then later: new_node.next = map.get(original_node.next); new_node.random = map.get(original_node.random).

examples.input: Original nodes: A, B, C with values 1,2,3
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with empty map {}. Step 2: For node A (val=1), create new node A' (val=1), map[A] = A'. Step 3: For node B (val=2), create B', map[B] = B'. Step 4: For node C (val=3), create C', map[C] = C'. Result map has 3 entries.

examples.input: Original nodes: X, Y with values 10,20, and X.random=Y, Y.random=null
examples.trace: Step 1: Assume map is {X: X', Y: Y'}. Step 2: For X', to set random, look up map[Y] which gives Y'. Step 3: For Y', to set random, look up map[null] which is null (handled by get method). Pointers set correctly.

testCases.input: Single node with val=5, map populated
Three nodes: A->B->C, map lookup for A.random=B
Node with random pointing to itself, map lookup
List with duplicate values but different nodes, map keys are node objects
testCases.expected: Each original node maps to a unique new node

testCases.input: Lookup for non-existent key (e.g., null if not handled)
testCases.expected: Should return null or handle error

testCases.input: Map used to set next pointer for node in middle

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two-Pass Traversal for Deep Copy
ladders.desc: Traverse the linked list twice: first to create nodes and map, second to set pointers.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that copying a linked list with random pointers requires two separate phases because the random pointers can create dependencies that aren't resolved in a single pass. In the first pass, we focus on creating all new nodes and building a mapping from original to new nodes. This ensures that every original node has a corresponding new node before we try to link them. In the second pass, we use this mapping to set up the next and random pointers for each new node by looking up the mapped counterparts. This two-pass strategy decouples node creation from pointer assignment, avoiding issues where a random pointer might point to a node that hasn't been created yet. The time complexity is O(n) for each pass, totaling O(n). Space complexity is O(1) extra per node for the new list, plus O(n) for the map, but the core logic here focuses on the traversal pattern. Connection to L1: This logic utilizes the hash map from L1 to enable the pointer setting in the second pass. Common mistakes include trying to do everything in one pass (which fails if random points forward) or not traversing all nodes in both passes. To avoid, always perform two complete traversals and ensure the map is fully populated before the second pass. Real code pattern: Pass 1: curr = head; while curr: new_node = Node(curr.val); map[curr] = new_node; curr = curr.next. Pass 2: curr = head; while curr: new_node = map[curr]; new_node.next = map.get(curr.next); new_node.random = map.get(curr.random); curr = curr.next. Return map[head].

examples.input: List: 1->2->3 with randoms: 1.random=3, 2.random=1, 3.random=2
examples.trace: Pass 1: Traverse 1,2,3; create new nodes N1,N2,N3; map = {1:N1, 2:N2, 3:N3}. Pass 2: Traverse 1,2,3 again; for 1: N1.next=map[2]=N2, N1.random=map[3]=N3; for 2: N2.next=map[3]=N3, N2.random=map[1]=N1; for 3: N3.next=map[null]=null, N3.random=map[2]=N2.

examples.input: List: A->B with randoms: A.random=B, B.random=A
examples.trace: Pass 1: Traverse A,B; create NA,NB; map = {A:NA, B:NB}. Pass 2: Traverse A,B; for A: NA.next=map[B]=NB, NA.random=map[B]=NB; for B: NB.next=map[null]=null, NB.random=map[A]=NA.

testCases.expected: Pass 1: create one node; Pass 2: set pointers

testCases.input: List: 1->2->3, all randoms null, two passes
testCases.expected: Pass 1: create 3 nodes; Pass 2: set next pointers, randoms null
Pass 1: create 2 nodes; Pass 2: set pointers correctly

testCases.input: List: 1->2->3->4, random: 1.random=4, 4.random=1, others null, two passes
testCases.expected: Pass 1: create 4 nodes; Pass 2: set pointers

testCases.input: List with cycle in next pointers (not allowed in problem), two passes
testCases.expected: Infinite loop if not handled, but problem assumes null-terminated
Pass 1: create nodes; Pass 2: set pointers

testCases.input: List: 1->2->3->2 (node 2 repeated), random: as given, two passes
testCases.expected: Pass 1: create 4 new nodes; Pass 2: set pointers using map

testCases.input: Large list of 100 nodes, two passes
List where random points to node not yet created in single pass, two passes avoid issue
testCases.expected: Two passes ensure all nodes created first

ladders.title: Building Block — Linked List Traversal with Node Creation
ladders.desc: Traverse a linked list and create new nodes for each original node.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to copy a linked list, we first need to replicate each node individually. This involves iterating through the original list from head to tail, and for each node, allocating memory for a new node with the same value. This building block is fundamental because it sets up the structure of the new list before we add pointers. During traversal, we use a pointer (e.g., 'curr') that starts at the head and moves to the next node until it reaches null. At each step, we create a new node using the original node's value. This ensures we have a one-to-one correspondence in terms of node count and values. The time complexity is O(n) for traversing n nodes, and space complexity is O(n) for the new nodes (but this is part of the output, not extra space). Connection to L2: This is the first pass in the two-pass traversal; it creates the nodes that will later be linked in the second pass. Common mistakes include not handling the null case (empty list), forgetting to move the pointer, or creating nodes with incorrect values. To avoid, always check if head is null at the start, update curr = curr.next in the loop, and copy node.val accurately. Real code pattern: new_nodes = []; curr = head; while curr: new_node = Node(curr.val); new_nodes.append(new_node); curr = curr.next; this stores new nodes for later use, but in the full solution, we'd use a map instead of a list.

examples.trace: Step 1: curr points to node 10. Create new node N10 (val=10). Step 2: curr moves to node 20. Create N20 (val=20). Step 3: curr moves to node 30. Create N30 (val=30). Step 4: curr becomes null, stop. Result: three new nodes N10, N20, N30.

examples.input: List: A->B->null
examples.trace: Step 1: curr points to node A. Create new node NA (val='A'). Step 2: curr moves to node B. Create NB (val='B'). Step 3: curr becomes null, stop. Result: two new nodes NA, NB.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Node Attributes in a Linked List
ladders.desc: Read the value, next pointer, and random pointer of a node in a linked list.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to work with a linked list, we need to understand how to access the data stored in each node. In this problem, each node has three attributes: a value (e.g., an integer or string), a next pointer that points to the next node in the sequence, and a random pointer that can point to any node or null. Accessing these attributes is a basic operation because it allows us to read the structure of the list and copy it. For example, to copy a node, we read its value to create a new node with the same value. To traverse the list, we use the next pointer to move from one node to the next. To set up the copy, we read the random pointer to know which node it points to in the original list. In code, if 'node' is a reference to a node object, we access node.val for the value, node.next for the next pointer, and node.random for the random pointer. It's crucial to handle null cases: if node.next is null, it means the end of the list; if node.random is null, it means no random pointer. The time complexity for accessing an attribute is O(1), as it's a direct memory reference. Space complexity is O(1) for the operation itself. Connection to L3: This operation is used during traversal and node creation in L3. Common mistakes include trying to access attributes on a null node (causing errors) or misunderstanding that node.next and node.random are references, not values. To avoid, always check if node is not null before accessing, and understand that these pointers refer to other node objects. Real code pattern: value = node.val; next_node = node.next; random_node = node.random; if node is not null.

examples.input: Node with val=7, next points to another node, random=null
examples.trace: Step 1: Given node N. Step 2: Access N.val, which returns 7. Step 3: Access N.next, which returns a reference to the next node (e.g., memory address). Step 4: Access N.random, which returns null. All accesses are O(1).

examples.input: Node with val='X', next=null, random points to itself
examples.trace: Step 1: Given node X. Step 2: X.val returns 'X'. Step 3: X.next returns null. Step 4: X.random returns a reference to X itself (same memory address).

testCases.input: Node val=20, next points to node with val=30, random=null
testCases.expected: val=20, next is node reference, random=null

testCases.input: Node val=1, next=null, random points to node with val=2
testCases.expected: val=1, next=null, random is reference to other node

testCases.input: Node val=5, next points to itself (circular), random=null
Node val='a', next points to node 'b', random points to node 'a'
Node with negative val=-5, next=null, random=null
Node val=0, next points to null, random points to null
Node val=100, next points to node with val=200, random points to node with val=100
Node val=3, next points to node with val=4, random points to node with val=5

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: Learn what a linked list node is, with value, next, and random pointers.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a linked list is a data structure where elements, called nodes, are connected in a sequence. Each node contains two main parts: data (often called 'val' for value) and pointers to other nodes. In this problem, each node has three attributes: 'val' stores the data (e.g., an integer), 'next' is a pointer to the next node in the list, and 'random' is an additional pointer that can point to any node in the list or be null. The 'next' pointer forms the linear order of the list, like a chain where each node points to the next one, and the last node's 'next' is null, indicating the end. The 'random' pointer adds complexity because it can create cross-references, pointing to nodes anywhere in the list, which makes copying challenging. Understanding this structure is foundational because it defines the problem: we need to create a new list where each new node has the same 'val', and its 'next' and 'random' pointers point to the corresponding new nodes, not the original ones. This is like making a deep copy where the relationships are preserved but in a separate memory space. The time to access a node's attributes is constant, O(1), and space per node is O(1) for the attributes. Connection to L4: This concept explains the attributes that are accessed in L4. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with their values (e.g., thinking 'next' stores a value instead of a reference) or not understanding that 'random' can be null. To avoid, visualize nodes as boxes with val inside and arrows for next and random, and practice drawing simple lists. Real code pattern: In Python, a node might be defined as a class: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.next = None; self.random = None. This creates a node object that can be linked to others.

examples.input: A simple list: node1 (val=1) points next to node2 (val=2), random=null
examples.trace: Step 1: Node1 has val=1. Step 2: Node1.next is a reference to node2. Step 3: Node1.random is null. Step 4: Node2 has val=2, next=null, random=null. This forms a list: 1 -> 2 -> null.

examples.input: Node with val=3, next=null, random points to itself
examples.trace: Step 1: Node has val=3. Step 2: Node.next is null, so it's the last node. Step 3: Node.random is a reference to the same node, so it points to itself. This means the random pointer creates a self-loop.

testCases.input: Two nodes: nodeA val=10 next points to nodeB, random=null; nodeB val=20 next=null random=nodeA
Node val=0, next points to itself, random=null
Node val=1, next=null, random points to node with val=2
Node val=99, next points to node with val=100, random points to null
Node val=7, next=null, random points to same node

approaches.name: In-Place Interweaving O(n)
approaches.summary: Interweave new nodes between original nodes in the list, set random pointers by copying from original nodes' random pointers, and then separate the two lists. This modifies the original structure temporarily without extra space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Copy Linked List with Random Pointers Using In-Place Interweaving
ladders.desc: Complete solution to copy a linked list with random pointers using O(1) extra space by interweaving nodes.
ladders.explanation: The in-place interweaving approach solves the deep copy problem for linked lists with random pointers in O(n) time with O(1) extra space. The key insight is that we can temporarily modify the original list structure to create a mapping between original and copied nodes without using a hash table. We interweave new nodes between original nodes, which gives us direct access to both original and copied nodes in adjacent positions. This adjacency allows us to set random pointers efficiently: for any original node, its random pointer's next node (the interweaved copy) is exactly the copy we need to point to. After setting all random pointers, we separate the two lists to restore the original structure and isolate the copy. The intuition works because the interweaving creates a physical mapping: original node A's copy is always at A.next, and original node B (pointed to by A.random) has its copy at B.next. This eliminates the need for extra space while maintaining the ability to correctly set random pointers. The algorithm has three phases: 1) Create interweaved copy nodes. 2) Set random pointers for copies using the interweaved structure. 3) Separate the lists. Each phase traverses the list once, giving O(n) time. Space is O(1) as we only use a few pointers. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like empty lists, not properly restoring original list pointers during separation, and incorrectly setting random pointers when random is null. The separation phase requires careful pointer manipulation to avoid losing nodes.

examples.input: List: 1->2->3->null, Random: 1.random=3, 2.random=1, 3.random=2
examples.trace:
Original: 1(rand=3)->2(rand=1)->3(rand=2)->null
Phase 1 - Interweave: Create copy nodes between originals
1->1'->2->2'->3->3'->null
Phase 2 - Set random pointers:
For original 1: random points to 3, so 1'.random = 3.next = 3'
For original 2: random points to 1, so 2'.random = 1.next = 1'
For original 3: random points to 2, so 3'.random = 2.next = 2'
Phase 3 - Separate lists:
Restore original: 1->2->3->null
Extract copy: 1'->2'->3'->null
Final copy has 1'.random=3', 2'.random=1', 3'.random=2'

examples.input: List: A->B->null, Random: A.random=B, B.random=A
examples.trace:
Original: A(rand=B)->B(rand=A)->null
Phase 1: A->A'->B->B'->null
Phase 2: A'.random = B.next = B', B'.random = A.next = A'
Phase 3: Original restored to A->B->null, copy extracted as A'->B'->null

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Separate Interweaved Linked Lists
ladders.desc: Extract two interweaved linked lists into separate lists while restoring the original.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the final and most delicate phase of the in-place interweaving approach. After creating copy nodes between original nodes and setting random pointers, we have a single list with pattern original1->copy1->original2->copy2->... We need to separate this into two independent lists: the restored original list and the new copy list. The intuition is that we can traverse the interweaved list and carefully rewire the next pointers. For each pair of original and copy nodes, we set original.next to skip over the copy (pointing to the next original), and copy.next to point to the next copy. This requires maintaining pointers to the current original, current copy, and the next nodes in sequence. The algorithm works because the interweaved structure guarantees that nodes alternate regularly. We iterate through the list, and for each original node O and its copy C (at O.next), we set O.next = C.next (which is either the next original or null). Then we set C.next = (C.next.next if C.next exists, else null). We must handle the end of the list carefully when there's no next pair. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse once. Space is O(1). Common mistakes include losing reference to the copy list's head, not handling null pointers at the end correctly, and modifying pointers in the wrong order causing infinite loops or lost nodes. The separation must preserve both lists completely.

examples.input: Interweaved: 1->1'->2->2'->3->3'->null
examples.trace:
Start: orig=1, copy=1', next_orig=2
Step1: 1.next = 1'.next = 2
Step2: 1'.next = 2.next = 2'
Move: orig=2, copy=2', next_orig=3
Step3: 2.next = 2'.next = 3
Step4: 2'.next = 3.next = 3'
Move: orig=3, copy=3', next_orig=null
Step5: 3.next = 3'.next = null
Step6: 3'.next = null
Result: Original:1->2->3->null, Copy:1'->2'->3'->null

examples.input: Interweaved: A->A'->B->B'->null
examples.trace:
Start: orig=A, copy=A', next_orig=B
Step1: A.next = A'.next = B
Step2: A'.next = B.next = B'
Move: orig=B, copy=B', next_orig=null
Step3: B.next = B'.next = null
Step4: B'.next = null
Result: Original:A->B->null, Copy:A'->B'->null

ladders.title: Core Logic — Setting Random Pointers Using Interweaved Structure
ladders.desc: How to correctly assign random pointers in copied nodes by leveraging the interweaved node arrangement.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique that makes the in-place approach work. After interweaving original and copy nodes (pattern: original->copy->original->copy...), we need to set the random pointers for all copy nodes. The key insight is that for any original node O, its random pointer points to some original node R. In the interweaved list, the copy of R is guaranteed to be at R.next. Therefore, the copy of O (which is at O.next) should have its random pointer point to R.next. This works because the interweaving creates a direct physical mapping: the copy of any node is always immediately after it. The algorithm traverses the interweaved list by jumping two steps at a time to process each original node. For each original node O, if O.random exists, we set O.next.random = O.random.next. If O.random is null, we set O.next.random = null. This correctly handles all cases. The intuition relies on the invariant that copy nodes are adjacent to their originals, so we can access any copy via its original's next pointer. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse half the nodes (the originals). Space is O(1). Common mistakes include not checking for null random pointers, incorrectly assuming the interweaved structure when it hasn't been fully created, or trying to access nodes that don't exist. This step must be performed after interweaving is complete and before separation begins.

examples.input: Interweaved: 1->1'->2->2'->3->3'->null, Original randoms: 1.random=3, 2.random=1, 3.random=2
examples.trace:
Traverse originals at positions 1,2,3:
For original 1: 1.random=3, so 1'.random = 3.next = 3'
For original 2: 2.random=1, so 2'.random = 1.next = 1'
For original 3: 3.random=2, so 3'.random = 2.next = 2'
All copy random pointers set correctly.

examples.input: Interweaved: A->A'->B->B'->null, Original randoms: A.random=B, B.random=A
examples.trace:
For original A: A.random=B, so A'.random = B.next = B'
For original B: B.random=A, so B'.random = A.next = A'
Result matches original relationships.

ladders.title: Building Block — Interweaving Nodes in a Linked List
ladders.desc: Insert new nodes between existing nodes of a linked list to create an alternating pattern.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves modifying a linked list by inserting new nodes between each pair of existing nodes. Given an original list A->B->C->..., we want to create A->A'->B->B'->C->C'->... where A', B', C' are new nodes (copies in our full problem). The intuition is straightforward: we traverse the original list, and for each node, we create a new node, insert it after the current node, then skip over both to continue. The algorithm maintains a current pointer. While current is not null, we store next node, create a copy node, set current.next to copy, set copy.next to stored next, then move current to the stored next to continue. This creates the interweaved pattern in one pass. Time complexity is O(n) as we process each node once. Space is O(1) for pointers, though creating new nodes allocates memory for the copy list separately. Common mistakes include losing the reference to the next original node after insertion, not handling the end of the list correctly, or creating cycles accidentally. This building block is crucial because it establishes the physical adjacency between original and copy nodes, enabling the random pointer copying without extra space.

examples.input: Original: 1->2->3->null
examples.trace:
Start: current=1, next=2
Create 1', set 1.next=1', 1'.next=2
Current moves to 2, next=3
Create 2', set 2.next=2', 2'.next=3
Current moves to 3, next=null
Create 3', set 3.next=3', 3'.next=null
Result: 1->1'->2->2'->3->3'->null

examples.input: Original: A->B->null
examples.trace:
Start: current=A, next=B
Create A', set A.next=A', A'.next=B
Current moves to B, next=null
Create B', set B.next=B', B'.next=null
Result: A->A'->B->B'->null

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Deep Copy of a Single Linked List Node
ladders.desc: Create an independent copy of a node with its value and pointers.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves creating a new node that is a deep copy of an existing node. In the context of linked lists with random pointers, each node has at least two fields: a value (often integer) and a random pointer (which may point to any node in the list or be null). A deep copy means the new node has the same value but its random pointer should eventually point to the corresponding copy of whatever the original's random points to (handled separately in higher levels). For now, we focus on creating the node with the value copied and pointers initialized to null. The intuition is simple: allocate memory for a new node, copy the value from the original, and set next and random to null initially. This operation is performed for every node in the list during the interweaving phase. Time complexity is O(1) per node. Space allocates new memory for the copy. Common mistakes include shallow copying (where pointers reference the same objects), not handling different data types correctly, or forgetting to initialize pointers. This operation is the atomic building block for creating the entire copy list.

examples.input: Node with value=5, random=some_node
examples.trace:
Original node: value=5, random points to some other node
Create new node: allocate memory
Set new_node.value = 5
Set new_node.next = null
Set new_node.random = null (to be set later)
Result is an independent node with same value.

examples.input: Node with value='A', random=null
examples.trace:
Original: value='A', random=null
Create new node
Set new_node.value = 'A'
Set new_node.next = null
Set new_node.random = null
Copy complete.

testCases.expected: New node with value=10, next=null, random=null
New node with value=20, next=null, random=null
New node with value=0, next=null, random=null
New node with value=-5, next=null, random=null
New node with value=100, next=null, random=null
New node with value='X', next=null, random=null
New node with value=3.14, next=null, random=null
New node with value=true, next=null, random=null
New node with value='hello', next=null, random=null
New node with value=null, next=null, random=null

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Linked List Node Structure and Traversal
ladders.desc: Understanding basic linked list nodes and how to traverse them sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a linked list node typically contains a value and a next pointer to the following node. In our problem, nodes also have a random pointer, but for foundation we focus on the basic structure. A node is often defined as a class with attributes like val, next, and random. Traversal means starting at the head (first node) and following next pointers until reaching null. The intuition is that linked lists are sequential data structures where each node points to the next, forming a chain. To traverse, we initialize a current pointer to head, then repeatedly move current = current.next until current becomes null. This allows us to access each node exactly once. Time complexity for traversing n nodes is O(n). Space is O(1) for the pointer. Common mistakes include infinite loops if there's a cycle (not in this problem), losing the head reference, or accessing null pointers. This foundation is essential because all operations in the higher levels—interweaving, setting randoms, separating—rely on traversing the list and manipulating pointers.

examples.input: List: 1->2->3->null
examples.trace:
Start: current = head (node 1)
Visit node 1, current = current.next = node 2
Visit node 2, current = current.next = node 3
Visit node 3, current = current.next = null
Traversal complete.

examples.input: List: A->B->null
examples.trace:
Start: current = head (node A)
Visit node A, current = current.next = node B
Visit node B, current = current.next = null
Done.

testCases.expected: Traversal visits nodes A, B, C
